result.markdown:
---
title: "Likelihood & Maximum Likelihood"
subtitle: "INFO 521 · Module 3 · Lecture A — From Loss to Likelihood"
author: "Greg Chism"
institute: "School of Information · University of Arizona"
date: last-modified
date-format: "MMMM YYYY"
---


## Learning outcomes {.smaller}

By the end of Lecture A you should be able to:

1. **State the linear-Gaussian noise model** and read it generatively.
2. **Write the likelihood** $p(\mathbf{y}\mid\mathbf{X},\mathbf{w},\sigma^2)$ and
   the log-likelihood $\ell(\mathbf{w})$.
3. **Derive the MLE** — and show $\hat{\mathbf{w}}_{\text{MLE}}$ **is** the
   least-squares $\hat{\mathbf{w}}$ from Module 1.
4. **Estimate the noise** — $\hat{\sigma}^2$ — and explain why it is **biased**.

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: the arc is loss → likelihood. Same fit, new foundation — and the
foundation buys two things the loss never gave us: σ̂² and (Lecture B) uncertainty.
:::

## Why squared error? (the debt from Module 1) {.smaller}

Module 1 *chose* the mean-squared-error loss

$$
\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}) = \tfrac{1}{N}\,\lVert \mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\mathbf{w}\rVert_2^2
$$

because it was smooth and penalized big misses. Reasonable — but a **choice**.

[Today we *earn* it: squared error is what a **Gaussian noise model** implies.
The accent hint on m1a's loss slide was this lecture.]{style="color: var(--accent)"}

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: (Clay 10, framing). Students accepted MSE on faith in M1; this
lecture replaces faith with a model. Pay the debt explicitly — it lands well.
:::

## The noise model {.smaller}

Assume each patient's systolic BP is the linear signal **plus Gaussian noise**:

$$
y_n = \mathbf{w}^{\!\top}\mathbf{x}_n + \varepsilon_n,
\qquad
\varepsilon_n \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\ \sigma^2)\ \ \text{independently.}
$$

Equivalently, each observation is a **draw from a Gaussian centered on the line**:

$$
p(y_n \mid \mathbf{x}_n, \mathbf{w}, \sigma^2)
= \mathcal{N}\!\left(y_n \,\middle|\, \mathbf{w}^{\!\top}\mathbf{x}_n,\ \sigma^2\right).
$$

This is a **generative story**: fix $\mathbf{w}, \sigma^2$, and the model can
*produce* datasets — the picture m3b's repeated-datasets schematic draws.

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: (Clay 10). Two readings of one equation: signal + noise, or a
conditional Gaussian per patient. The generative reading powers everything in M3.
:::

## The Gaussian, in one slide {.smaller}

<!-- CUTTABLE: prereq recap (async L09b). Drop first if the deck runs long. -->

$$
\mathcal{N}(y \mid \mu, \sigma^2)
= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}}
  \exp\!\left(-\frac{(y-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)
$$

::: {#cell-fig-gaussian .cell execution_count=2}

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display}
![The Gaussian (illustrative): mean μ sets the center, σ sets the spread — ~68% within ±1σ, ~95% within ±2σ.](m3a-likelihood-mle_files/figure-revealjs/fig-gaussian-output-1.svg){#fig-gaussian fig-alt='Illustrative Gaussian probability density curve centered at mu with the one- and two-standard-deviation intervals shaded in progressively lighter blue; roughly 68 percent of the area lies within one standard deviation and 95 percent within two.'}
:::
:::


::: {.notes}
Speaker note: (Clay 09b, compressed to one slide — the rest of probability review
is async prereq). Only two facts matter today: μ centers it, σ scales it.
:::

## The likelihood {.smaller}

The patients are independent, so the probability of the **whole dataset** is a
product — read as a **function of the parameters** with the data fixed:

$$
p(\mathbf{y}\mid\mathbf{X},\mathbf{w},\sigma^2)
= \prod_{n=1}^{N} \mathcal{N}\!\left(y_n \,\middle|\, \mathbf{w}^{\!\top}\mathbf{x}_n,\ \sigma^2\right)
$$

::: {.fragment}
- Data $(\mathbf{X}, \mathbf{y})$: **fixed** — it already happened.
- Parameters $(\mathbf{w}, \sigma^2)$: **free** — the knobs we turn.
- **The likelihood scores the knobs**: parameters under which the observed data
  are *more probable* score higher.
:::

::: {.fragment}
[**Maximum likelihood**: pick the parameters that make the data you actually saw
as probable as possible.]{style="color: var(--accent)"}
:::

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: (Clay 10/11). The single most common confusion: a likelihood is not
a distribution over parameters (that's Bayes, Module 4) — it is a *score*.
:::

## The log-likelihood {.smaller}

Products of exponentials beg for a logarithm — we write the log-likelihood
$\ell(\mathbf{w}) = \log p(\mathbf{y}\mid\mathbf{X},\mathbf{w},\sigma^2)$,
reserving $\mathcal{L}$ for losses:

$$
\ell(\mathbf{w}) = -\tfrac{N}{2}\log 2\pi \;-\; N\log\sigma
\;-\; \tfrac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{n=1}^{N}\left(y_n - \mathbf{w}^{\!\top}\mathbf{x}_n\right)^2
$$

::: {.fragment}
Look at the $\mathbf{w}$-dependent part: it is **$-$(sum of squared errors),
scaled**. Maximizing $\ell$ in $\mathbf{w}$ *is* minimizing squared error:

$$
\arg\max_{\mathbf{w}} \ell(\mathbf{w})
\;=\;
\arg\min_{\mathbf{w}} \mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}).
$$
:::

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: log is monotone, so it preserves the argmax; it turns the product
into a sum and the exponential into the quadratic students already know. The
equivalence is visible before any calculus — point at the third term.
:::

## The likelihood surface over NHANES

::: {#cell-fig-loglik-profile .cell execution_count=3}

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display}
![ℓ(w₁) for SBP on standardized age (intercept at ŵ₀): the peak IS the Module-1 least-squares slope.](m3a-likelihood-mle_files/figure-revealjs/fig-loglik-profile-output-1.svg){#fig-loglik-profile fig-alt='Profile of the log-likelihood as a function of the slope parameter w1 for the NHANES systolic-blood-pressure-on-age model, with the intercept held at its estimate. The curve is a smooth concave arch peaking at the maximum-likelihood slope of about 6.5 mmHg per standard deviation of age, marked with a vermillion vertical line; the least-squares estimate from Module 1 sits at exactly the same spot.'}
:::
:::


::: {.notes}
Speaker note: one smooth concave arch — the 1-D shadow of m1b's convex loss bowl,
flipped. The peak lands on 6.48, the same number from m1a's fitted line.
:::

## MLE for w: an old friend {.smaller}

Set the gradient of $\ell$ in $\mathbf{w}$ to zero:

$$
\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \mathbf{w}}
= \tfrac{1}{\sigma^2}\left(\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}\mathbf{w}\right)
= \mathbf{0}
\qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad
\boxed{\;\hat{\mathbf{w}}_{\text{MLE}} = \left(\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}\right)^{-1}\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{y}\;}
$$

The **normal equations again** — $\sigma^2$ cancels entirely.

[**Least squares = maximum likelihood under Gaussian noise.** Same $\hat{\mathbf{w}}$,
better foundation: now it comes from a model, not a preference.]{style="color: var(--accent)"}

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: (Clay 10/11 — the punchline). Nothing new to memorize: the
derivation IS m1b's, wearing likelihood clothes. What's new is the *interpretation*
— and the two dividends on the next slides.
:::

## Dividend 1: the noise gets estimated too {.smaller}

The loss treated the scatter around the line as a nuisance. The likelihood makes
it a **parameter**. Setting $\partial\ell/\partial\sigma = 0$:

$$
\hat{\sigma}^2
= \tfrac{1}{N}\sum_{n=1}^{N}\left(y_n - \hat{\mathbf{w}}^{\!\top}\mathbf{x}_n\right)^2
= \tfrac{1}{N}\left(\mathbf{y}^{\!\top}\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{y}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}\hat{\mathbf{w}}\right)
$$

— the **average squared residual** at the fitted line.

For SBP on standardized age: $\hat{\sigma} \approx 15.9$ mmHg — the model's honest
"give or take" for one patient.

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: (Clay 11). This is exactly the σ̂² formula m3b's recap slide quotes.
Clinical read: predictions carry ±16 mmHg of patient-level scatter — age explains
the trend, not the person.
:::

## Model check: are the residuals Gaussian-ish?

::: {#cell-fig-residuals .cell execution_count=4}

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display}
![NHANES residuals vs. the fitted 𝒩(0, σ̂²): bell-ish, but right-skewed — models are assumptions, check them.](m3a-likelihood-mle_files/figure-revealjs/fig-residuals-output-1.svg){#fig-residuals fig-alt='Histogram of the residuals from the NHANES systolic-blood-pressure-on-age fit, shown as a density, with the fitted Gaussian curve with mean zero and standard deviation about 16 mmHg overlaid in vermillion. The histogram is roughly bell-shaped but visibly right-skewed, with a heavier tail of large positive residuals than the Gaussian predicts.'}
:::
:::


::: {.notes}
Speaker note: honest data moment — the Gaussian is decent in the bulk but the
right tail (hypertensive spikes) is heavier. The assumption is *useful*, not
*true*; binarizing that tail is exactly what Project 2 / Module 5 does.
:::

## Dividend 2 — with a catch: $\hat{\sigma}^2$ is biased {.smaller}

Averaging over repeated datasets from the generating process:

$$
\mathbb{E}\!\left[\hat{\sigma}^2\right] = \frac{N - D}{N}\,\sigma^2 \;<\; \sigma^2
\qquad(D\ \text{fitted parameters})
$$

- The fit **chases the sample**: residuals from $\hat{\mathbf{w}}$ are
  *systematically smaller* than the true errors from $\mathbf{w}$.
- Each fitted parameter eats one *degree of freedom* — hence the $N{-}D$.
- The classic repair divides by $N{-}D$ instead of $N$; at $N \approx 5{,}102$,
  $D = 2$, the difference is invisible — at $N = 10$ it is not.

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: (Clay 11 — the bias example). Contrast with Lecture B's result that
$\hat{\mathbf{w}}$ is unbiased: MLE does NOT hand you unbiasedness for free; you
check it per estimator. That contrast is the point of the next figure.
:::

## Seeing the bias

::: {#cell-fig-sigma-bias .cell execution_count=5}

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display}
![Mean σ̂² over 2,000 NHANES subsamples per size: small samples systematically UNDER-estimate the noise, tracking (N−2)/N · σ².](m3a-likelihood-mle_files/figure-revealjs/fig-sigma-bias-output-1.svg){#fig-sigma-bias fig-alt='Average of the maximum-likelihood noise-variance estimate across two thousand repeated NHANES subsamples, plotted against subsample size from 4 to 60 as a blue line with circular markers. The average sits well below the full-sample sigma-hat-squared reference line (vermillion, dashed) at small sizes and climbs toward it as the subsample grows, closely tracking the theoretical (N minus 2 over N) bias curve drawn in green.'}
:::
:::


::: {.notes}
Speaker note: real NHANES resampling, no synthetic data. The empirical curve rides
the (N−2)/N line almost exactly — theory you can watch happen. Fixed seed 521.
:::

## What maximum likelihood buys {.smaller}

One framework, three purchases:

1. **A principled loss.** Squared error stops being a taste and becomes a
   *consequence* (of Gaussian noise). Different noise model ⇒ different loss.
2. **Noise as a first-class estimate.** $\hat{\sigma}^2$ quantifies patient-level
   scatter — the loss view had nowhere to put it.
3. **A road to uncertainty.** The *curvature* of $\ell$ at the peak says how
   sharply the data pin down $\hat{\mathbf{w}}$ — that is **Lecture B**.

And one caution: MLE estimators are not automatically unbiased ($\hat{\sigma}^2$
wasn't) — properties get **checked**, not assumed.

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: consolidation slide. If students remember one line: same fit,
stronger story, plus σ̂² — and the curvature tease sets up Lecture B verbatim.
:::

## Recap & bridge {.smaller}

- **Noise model**: $y_n = \mathbf{w}^{\!\top}\mathbf{x}_n + \varepsilon_n$,
  $\ \varepsilon_n \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$.
- **Likelihood → $\ell(\mathbf{w})$**: maximizing it **is** minimizing
  $\mathcal{L}$ — least squares, re-earned.
- **$\hat{\sigma}^2 = \tfrac{1}{N}\sum_n r_n^2$** — biased by $(N{-}D)/N$; repair
  with $N{-}D$.

[**Next (Lecture B):** how *uncertain* is $\hat{\mathbf{w}}$? The Hessian of
$\ell$ — the same $\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}$ yet again — becomes the Fisher
information, and uncertainty gets a formula.]{style="color: var(--accent)"}

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: hand off to m3b exactly where it picks up ("the MLE derived in the
likelihood lecture of this module"). The through-line object is XᵀX: loss bowl
(M1), likelihood peak (here), Fisher information (m3b), Laplace posterior (M5).
:::
